segments.title: Blackett Strait rewards prepared information use
segments.summary: The Battle of Blackett Strait lecture frames preparation and information use as the reason a routine Japanese resupply ran into an overwhelming American night action.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing lesson. Dr. Clarke opens by contrasting a Japanese resupply withdrawal with an American force that knew contact was likely, had heavier firepower, and assumed the enemy might be acting effectively. The result is framed less as an even fight than as a case study in why commanders prepare for the dangerous enemy course of action.

evidence.note: The opening identifies Blackett Strait and contrasts an American bombardment force that expects Japanese contact with Japanese destroyers returning from a routine resupply mission.
The framing says the Japanese never got a chance to fight back, being outmaneuvered, outnumbered, and outgunned by American destroyers and cruisers.
The stated lesson is to presume the enemy has useful information and may act correctly, so the force is ready when events develop dangerously.
